goal: Review the proof and issues for rm-08094; agree on the key remaining gaps and an action plan.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Let me open by reframing this in the language I trust. We want to show the family $\mathcal{A}$ is Hurwitz-stable iff the four Kharitonov vertices are. The natural matrix-theoretic engine here is the **Lyapunov inequality**: $A$ is Hurwitz iff there exists $P \succ 0$ with $A^T P + P A \prec 0$. So the real question is: can we find a *common* certificate, or a structured family of certificates, that propagates stability from the four vertices to the whole polytope of companion matrices?

A few sharp points:

1. **The companion structure is everything.** The set $\mathcal{A}$ is an affine image of the coefficient box — it's a convex polytope of matrices with $2^n$ vertices, but they only differ in the last row. The miracle of Kharitonov is that *four* of those $2^n$ vertices suffice. Matrix-theoretically, this must come from a sign/interlacing structure in how the last row perturbs the spectrum. I'd want to know: what is the eigenvalue-perturbation behavior as we move $a_i$ along an edge of the box? Along an edge, only one coefficient varies, so $A(t) = A_0 + t\, e_n e_i^T$ — a **rank-one** perturbation path.

2. This rank-one structure is exactly where my instincts fire. Eigenvalues under a rank-one update move monotonically in a way governed by a barrier-type function. If $\det(sI - A(t)) = p_0(s) + t\,s^i$, then on the imaginary axis $s=i\omega$ the real and imaginary parts split the even and odd coefficients — that's the algebraic shadow of why four sign patterns (even-low/odd-high etc.) are extremal.

3. **What's the convexity statement?** For the extension to general matrix polytopes that the problem demands, we cannot rely on companion structure. So I want to isolate: is there a convex set of Lyapunov solutions $P$ over the polytope? Stability of a polytope of matrices is *not* in general implied by stability of vertices — that's false (the Bialas counterexample). So the companion/interlacing structure is doing essential work, and we must name exactly what property of $\mathcal{A}$ replaces "vertex stability $\Rightarrow$ polytope stability."

So my question to the room: **where is the proof currently using the rank-one edge structure, and does it produce an explicit Lyapunov certificate or just an eigenvalue-crossing argument?** Crossing arguments (an eigenvalue can only leave the left half-plane by crossing the imaginary axis) are clean but I want the quadratic-form version, because that's what generalizes.

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan, you've put your finger on the central tension, and I want to sharpen it from the side of *which* convexity is actually operative. Let me push back gently on one framing: the eigenvalue-crossing argument is not merely a poor cousin of the Lyapunov certificate — for *this* family it is the honest carrier of the structure, and I think the right proof keeps both in play.

Here is the picture I'd insist on:

1. **The boundary-crossing principle as a homotopy invariant.** The family $\mathcal{A}$ is a *connected* (indeed convex) set of matrices. Connectivity means: if some matrix in $\mathcal{A}$ is unstable while another is stable, then along any path between them an eigenvalue crosses the imaginary axis. So instability of the family is detected *purely on the boundary* $\{A \in \mathcal{A} : A \text{ has an eigenvalue } i\omega\}$. This is a topological statement — a one-parameter deformation argument — and it does not need companion structure to be *stated*. What companion structure buys us is a parametrization of that boundary.

2. **Where the four vertices come from — the even/odd splitting as a bilinear form.** Take $s = i\omega$. The condition $\det(i\omega I - A) = 0$ for a companion matrix is $(i\omega)^n + \sum a_k (i\omega)^k = 0$. Split into the real part $P_{\text{even}}(\omega) = \sum_{k \text{ even}} \pm a_k \omega^k$ and imaginary part $P_{\text{odd}}(\omega)$. A boundary matrix exists in $\mathcal{A}$ at frequency $\omega$ iff $0$ lies in the box-image of the pair $(P_{\text{even}}, P_{\text{odd}})$. But $P_{\text{even}}$ depends only on even-indexed $a_k$ and $P_{\text{odd}}$ only on odd-indexed — *the two coordinates of the obstruction are functions of disjoint variable sets*. That decoupling is precisely a rank-structure / tensor-product fact, and it is why the extreme configurations are the **four** sign-corners, not all $2^n$. This is the matrix-theoretic content I'd want stated as a lemma about the image of $\mathcal{A}$ under the map $A \mapsto$ (real, imag) of $\det(i\omega I - A)$.

3. **The Lyapunov side — and why a single $P$ won't do.** You're right to cite Bialas: vertex stability does *not* imply polytope stability in general, so any common-$P$ dream is dead a priori. The correct generalizable object is not one $P$ but a *parameter-affine family* $P(A)$, i.e. a quadratic certificate that varies **affinely or rationally** over the polytope. The known healthy class is exactly the one where the eigenvalue-crossing set has the rank-one/edge monotonicity Dan named: along $A(t) = A_0 + t e_n e_i^T$, the Hermitian form $A(t)^* P + P A(t)$ moves by the rank-one $t(P e_n e_i^T + e_i e_n^T P)$, and monotonicity of the relevant Rayleigh quotient is what closes the argument. So I'd phrase the action item as: **identify the property "the crossing set is a multiaffine image with disjoint coordinate dependence" and prove that property alone yields edge-stability $\Rightarrow$ polytope-stability.**

4. **My structural instinct.** This whole thing smells like an *interval/polytope analog of

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Good — Dan and László have isolated the right object, but let me inject the perspective I always come back to: *compute the first nontrivial case by hand and watch where the structure forces itself*.

Take $n=2$, $p(s) = s^2 + a_1 s + a_0$. The crossing condition at $s=i\omega$ splits as
$$\underbrace{-\omega^2 + a_0}_{\text{even, real}} + i\,\underbrace{a_1\omega}_{\text{odd, imag}} = 0.$$
The even part involves only $a_0$, the odd only $a_1$ — László's disjointness in its purest form. Stability of the box reduces to $a_0^- > 0$ and $a_1^- > 0$, i.e. *two* corners, not $2^2=4$. The count "four" is the $n\ge 3$ phenomenon where the even polynomial $a_0 - a_2\omega^2 + \dots$ has *interlacing* sign demands across frequencies. So the real theorem behind Kharitonov is an **interlacing statement** — Hermite–Biehler — and interlacing is exactly the language I live in from the Selberg trace formula and the GUE story: zeros of even/odd parts interlace iff the polynomial is Hurwitz, precisely as eigenvalues of a matrix and its rank-one-bordered minor interlace.

Now the matrix-theoretic engine I'd push, beyond Lyapunov:

1. **The crossing set as a spectral curve.** Consider $A \in \mathcal{A}$ with $A v = i\omega v$. Write $v = (1, i\omega, \dots, (i\omega)^{n-1})^T$ — for companion matrices the eigenvector is *explicit*, the Vandermonde vector. Then the only nontrivial equation is the last row: $\sum a_k (i\omega)^k = -(i\omega)^n$. So the boundary is parametrized by a *single real frequency* $\omega$, and the box-image obstruction lives in $\mathbb{R}^2$ via $(\mathrm{Re}, \mathrm{Im})$. That's why this is so much more rigid than a general matrix polytope — the eigenvector is frozen.

2. **The monotonicity is Rayleigh-quotient monotonicity.** Along $A(t) = A_0 + t e_n e_i^T$, test the Lyapunov form against the *fixed* Vandermonde direction. The variation is rank-one in exactly the coordinate $a_i$, and its sign is determined by the parity of $i$ — even vs. odd $\omega^i$. This is the quadratic-form shadow of Hermite–Biehler. I claim the clean lemma is: **the map $\omega \mapsto (\text{Re}, \text{Im})$ of the characteristic equation has each coordinate monotone-in-each-variable with a fixed sign pattern**, and four sign-corners suffice to bound the image because the two coordinates are functions of disjoint variables (László) *each of which is monotone* (the missing ingredient).

So my pointed question to the room: **does the current proof use the explicit Vandermonde eigenvector?** Because if it does, the "extension to general polytopes" the problem demands is *hopeless in that exact form* — Bialas already kills it — and the honest generalizable statement is the interlacing/Hermite–Biehler one dressed as eigenvector-frozen rank-one monotonicity. Let me ask: is this *really* a Kharitonov phenomenon, or is it an interlacing phenomenon that Kharitonov happened to disc

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Peter, you've just handed us the key, and I want to make it explicit and algorithmic. The frozen Vandermonde eigenvector $v_\omega = (1, i\omega, \dots, (i\omega)^{n-1})^T$ is the entire game, and yes — any honest "extension to general polytopes" must abandon it, because Bialas is a theorem and we don't get to repeal it. So let me state what I think the two-tier result actually is.

**Tier 1 (the companion miracle, via frozen eigenvector).** Because the eigenvector is frozen, the crossing set is a curve in $\mathbb{R}^2$ parametrized by $\omega$:
$$ \Gamma(\omega) = \big(\mathrm{Re}\,q(\omega),\ \mathrm{Im}\,q(\omega)\big), \quad q(\omega) = (i\omega)^n + \sum_k a_k (i\omega)^k. $$
The box of coefficients maps to a *rectangle* in $\mathbb{R}^2$ at each $\omega$ — and here is the precise statement combining László's disjointness with Peter's monotonicity: the rectangle $R(\omega) = [P_{\text{even}}^-(\omega), P_{\text{even}}^+(\omega)] \times [P_{\text{odd}}^-(\omega), P_{\text{odd}}^+(\omega)]$ has its **four corners realized by exactly the four sign-patterns** $M_0$. The family is unstable iff $0 \in R(\omega)$ for some $\omega \ge 0$ (plus the trivial $a_0 > 0$ at $\omega=0$). And $0 \in R(\omega)$ for the box iff $0$ is "boxed in" by the four corner curves — which is precisely Hurwitz-stability of the four vertex matrices. That's the proof, and it's *constructive*: I can certify instability by solving for the $\omega$ where a corner curve crosses an axis.

**Tier 2 (the generalizable engine — quadratic forms).** Now I disagree slightly with the resignation in the room. The Lyapunov picture *is* recoverable here, and it's the right vehicle for generalization — we just must not ask for a single $P$. Here is the clean lemma. For a companion matrix, take the Lyapunov form against the frozen direction $v_\omega$:
$$ v_\omega^* (A^T P + P A) v_\omega. $$
Along an edge $A(t) = A_0 + t\, e_n e_i^T$, the variation is the **rank-one** Hermitian perturbation $t\,(P v_\omega)(e_i^* v_\omega)^* + \text{h.c.}$, and its sign is locked by the parity of $i$ — that is Peter's monotonicity made into a Rayleigh-quotient inequality. So I'd phrase the generalizable theorem as:

> If a matrix polytope admits a *frequency-parametrized family of test vectors* $v_\omega$ such that (a) the crossing condition is detected by $v_\omega^*(\cdot)v_\omega$, and (b) the form is monotone in each coordinate direction with disjoint coordinate dependence, then edge-stability $\Rightarrow$ polytope-stability, and a bounded set of sign-corners suffices.

That is the property that *replaces* "vertex stability $\Rightarrow$ polytope stability" — exactly what I asked for in my opening. It's the matrix analog of leverage scores: each coordinate's "importance" to the boundary is measured by $|e_i

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan, Peter — I think we have now converged on the correct two-tier picture, but let me name the structure of Tier 2 precisely, because I believe it is cleaner than "monotone in each coordinate with disjoint dependence," and the cleaner statement is what makes the extension theorem provable rather than aspirational.

1. **The crossing set is a multiaffine image, and that is the operative fact.** The map $\Phi: \prod_i [a_i^-, a_i^+] \to \mathbb{R}^2$, $a \mapsto (\mathrm{Re}\,q(\omega), \mathrm{Im}\,q(\omega))$ at fixed $\omega$, is *affine in each $a_i$ separately* — indeed it is affine in $a$ jointly here, but the disjointness Peter isolated makes it a **product of two independent affine maps** on disjoint variable blocks. A multiaffine map sends a box to a set whose support function is realized at the vertices; combined with the product structure, the image is exactly the rectangle $R(\omega)$ whose four corners are the four sign-patterns. This is the honest reason "four" appears — it is the vertex-realization property of multiaffine maps, not monotonicity per se. Monotonicity is a *consequence* of fixed-sign affine coefficients, but the load-bearing hypothesis is multiaffinity + coordinate-block decoupling.

2. **Edge-convexity is the generalizable replacement for "vertex $\Rightarrow$ polytope."** Here is the lemma I would prove and that survives Bialas. Define the *crossing functional* $c(A,\omega) = \det(A - i\omega I)$, or its real/imaginary parts. For a general matrix polytope, $0 \notin \overline{\mathrm{conv}}\{c(\text{vertices})\}$ at every $\omega$ is what fails. But if there is a frequency-parametrized test family $v_\omega$ with $c(A,\omega) = v_\omega^*(A - i\omega I)v_\omega$ that is multiaffine in the matrix entries restricted to the polytope's *defining facets*, then the image is a polytope in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with vertices at the matrix-vertices, and exclusion of $0$ is an edge-by-edge check. The companion case is the degenerate one where the test vector is frozen (Vandermonde), so the multiaffinity is automatic. So I would state Tier 2 as: **the property that replaces vertex-implies-polytope is "the crossing functional, against a fixed-direction-per-frequency test family, is multiaffine over the polytope."**

3. **Why I trust this framing — it is the same phenomenon as my $\theta$-function.** When I introduced $\theta(G)$, the point was that a *semidefinite* relaxation captures combinatorial data that linear vertex-enumeration cannot. Here the structure is dual: we have a quadratic form $v_\omega^*(A^TP + PA)v_\omega$ whose *vertex behavior* is faithful precisely because the test direction is held fixed — the SDP collapses to an LP on the boundary. That collapse is the matrix-theoretic content of Kharitonov. The Bialas counterexample is exactly a polytope where no such fixed-per-frequency test family exists, so the SDP does *not* collapse.

4. **One caution and an action item.** Peter is right that this is fundamentally Hermite–Biehler interlacing wearing matrix clothing. But interlacing of even/odd parts is itself the statement that the two affine-image curves $\

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Good — László's collapse-of-the-SDP-to-an-LP is exactly the right slogan, and I want to nail down the interlacing because that's where I think we still have a genuine gap, not just a framing choice.

Here is the subtle point Dan's Tier 1 glides over. Saying "the four corner curves box in the origin" is *not yet* a proof — you have to know the corner curves wind around the origin in the right order as $\omega$ runs from $0$ to $\infty$. That ordering **is** the interlacing of the zeros of $P_{\text{even}}(\omega)$ and $P_{\text{odd}}(\omega)$. Let me state the lemma I want proved purely matrix-theoretically:

1. **Interlacing as a Cauchy-type rank-one fact.** For the companion matrix, $\det(i\omega I - A) = P_{\text{even}}(\omega^2) + i\omega\,P_{\text{odd}}(\omega^2)$. Hurwitz stability is equivalent to the *real* roots of $P_{\text{even}}$ and $P_{\text{odd}}$ interlacing on $(0,\infty)$ — this is Hermite–Biehler. Now: interlacing of $P_{\text{even}}, P_{\text{odd}}$ is *the same* statement as eigenvalue interlacing between a symmetric matrix and a rank-one-bordered principal submatrix, via the Cauchy interlacing theorem. That is the matrix-theoretic dressing László wants — not metaphorically.

2. **Why this rescues Tier 1 from circularity.** Dan, your construction certifies "0 not boxed at any fixed $\omega$," but the topological content — that the boundary curve $\Gamma(\omega)$ has *winding number zero* about the origin iff the four vertices are stable — requires the monotone phase increase of $\arg q(\omega)$, which is *exactly* interlacing (the argument principle = Hermite–Biehler). So the honest theorem is: **frozen-eigenvector multiaffinity gives the rectangle, but interlacing gives the winding.** Both are needed.

3. **The real question — is this a Ramanujan/GUE phenomenon?** No, and that's worth saying out loud. Here the interlacing is *rigid and deterministic* — the zeros of $P_{\text{even}}, P_{\text{odd}}$ alternate perfectly. That's the opposite of GUE level repulsion, which is a *statistical* interlacing. The companion miracle is a degenerate, frozen version of the random-matrix interlacing I study; the SDP collapses precisely *because* there's no level-repulsion randomness — one frozen Vandermonde direction governs everything. So when Dan asks for the generalizable engine, my warning stands: the moment your test family $v_\omega$ stops being a *single* frozen direction and acquires genuine spectral spread, you're back in the regime where interlacing is statistical, the SDP does not collapse, and Bialas wins.

Action item I'd add: **prove the winding/argument-principle statement directly from Cauchy interlacing of the bordered companion submatrix**, so that Tier 1 is a theorem and not a picture. Let me check $n=3$ by hand to make sure the corner-curve ordering matches the interlacing claim — I bet the $a_0 a_3 < a_1 a_2$ Hurwitz determinant condition falls right out of it.

messages.author: coordinator
messages.body:
**Action plan synthesized** (7 steps):

Prove the Kharitonov theorem via a two-tier matrix-theoretic framework: Tier 1 uses the frozen Vandermonde eigenvector to reduce the crossing set to a multiaffine box-image whose four corners are the Kharitonov vertices, with interlacing/winding closing the argument; Tier 2 isolates the multiaffinity-of-the-crossing-functional property as the generalizable replacement for the false 'vertex stability implies polytope stability'.

plan.summary: Prove the Kharitonov theorem via a two-tier matrix-theoretic framework: Tier 1 uses the frozen Vandermonde eigenvector to reduce the crossing set to a multiaffine box-image whose four corners are the Kharitonov vertices, with interlacing/winding closing the argument; Tier 2 isolates the multiaffinity-of-the-crossing-functional property as the generalizable replacement for the false 'vertex stability implies polytope stability'.

steps.title: Establish the frozen Vandermonde eigenvector and crossing-curve parametrization
steps.body: For a companion matrix A in the family, verify that any imaginary eigenvalue i*omega has the explicit eigenvector v_omega = (1, i*omega, ..., (i*omega)^(n-1))^T. Show the boundary (crossing) condition reduces to the single last-row equation sum a_k (i*omega)^k = -(i*omega)^n, giving the curve Gamma(omega) = (Re q(omega), Im q(omega)) parametrized by one real frequency. This freezes the eigenvector and makes the obstruction live in R^2.

steps.title: Prove the even/odd disjoint-variable splitting and multiaffine box-to-rectangle lemma
steps.body: Decompose q(i*omega) into real part P_even(omega) (depending only on even-indexed coefficients) and imaginary part P_odd(omega) (only odd-indexed). Prove the map Phi: box -> R^2 at fixed omega is multiaffine and, by disjointness of variable blocks, factors as a product of two independent affine maps. Conclude the image is exactly the rectangle R(omega) = [P_even^-, P_even^+] x [P_odd^-, P_odd^+] whose four corners are realized by exactly four sign-pattern (Kharitonov) vertices.

steps.title: Prove interlacing and the winding-number statement via Cauchy interlacing
steps.body: Establish Hermite-Biehler: Hurwitz stability is equivalent to the real roots of P_even and P_odd interlacing on (0, infinity). Dress this as a matrix statement: interlacing equals eigenvalue interlacing between a symmetric matrix and its rank-one-bordered principal submatrix via the Cauchy interlacing theorem. Then prove via the argument principle that the boundary curve Gamma(omega) has winding number zero about the origin iff the four vertices are stable, supplying the monotone phase increase that the box-cornering argument alone does not give.

steps.title: Assemble the Tier 1 Kharitonov theorem (rectangle + winding)
steps.body: Combine the multiaffine rectangle lemma (step 1) with the interlacing/winding lemma (step 2) to give a complete, non-circular proof that the family A is Hurwitz-stable iff the four Kharitonov sign-corner vertices are stable. Make the instability certificate constructive: identify the frequency omega where a corner curve crosses an axis. Include the trivial omega=0 condition a_0 > 0.

steps.title: Verify the n=2 and n=3 base cases by hand
steps.body: Check n=2 (s^2 + a_1 s + a_0): confirm the crossing splits as (-omega^2 + a_0) + i(a_1 omega), reducing to two corners a_0^- > 0 and a_1^- > 0. Check n=3: confirm the corner-curve ordering matches the interlacing claim and that the Hurwitz determinant condition a_0 a_3 < a_1 a_2 falls out of the interlacing/winding analysis. Confirm the count 'four' is genuinely an n>=3 phenomenon.

steps.title: Formulate Tier 2 generalizable engine: multiaffine crossing functional as SDP-collapse
steps.body: State and prove the generalizable theorem replacing 'vertex stability => polytope stability': if a matrix polytope admits a frequency-parametrized test family v_omega such that the crossing functional c(A,omega) = v_omega^*(A - i*omega I)v_omega is multiaffine over the polytope's defining facets with disjoint coordinate-block dependence, then the crossing-set image is a polytope in R^2 with matrix-vertices as vertices, exclusion of 0 is an edge-by-edge check, and edge-stability implies polytope-stability. Frame this as the quadratic-form Lyapunov view v_omega^*(A^T P + P A)v_omega collapsing from SDP to LP because the test direction is fixed per frequency.

steps.title: Delimit scope against Bialas and the statistical-interlacing barrier
steps.body: Critically examine the boundary of Tier 2: confirm via the Bialas counterexample that no single common P exists and that vertex stability fails for general polytopes. Argue that the Tier 2 hypothesis precisely characterizes the cases where a fixed-per-frequency test family exists, and that once v_omega acquires genuine spectral spread the interlacing becomes statistical (GUE-like level repulsion), the SDP no longer collapses, and Bialas wins. State explicitly that the frozen Vandermonde direction cannot be retained in any honest extension.
